primary_diagnosis.name: POTS secondary to hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome - acute flare

pathophysiology_summary: In hEDS, lax connective tissue allows excessive venous distensibility and pooling, driving secondary POTS with orthostatic tachycardia, gray-out and fatigue that settle supine, alongside hypermobility-related diffuse myofascial pain and joint instability. This mimics cardiogenic syncope, occult GI bleeding (hypovolaemia) and acute dislocation. Reassuring features are strictly postural tachycardia settling supine, no melaena/haematemesis with normal supine BP, no current dislocation with intact neurovascular status, and no true syncope/chest pain. However, telehealth cannot obtain an ECG, examine the joints, or measure postural vitals, and the combined joint instability and orthostasis create a very high fall risk. The correct action is urgent in-person review (ECG + joints) plus fall-safety and supportive measures (abdominal binder, salt/fluids, bracing, seated activities) - not remote rate-control/vasoactive drugs - with 000 for GI bleeding, a locked dislocation, true syncope or chest pain.

differential.evidence_basis: Postural tachycardia with hypermobility-related pain on a known background.
Screen rest palpitations/chest pain/true syncope; ECG.
Postural-only tachycardia settling supine, no bleeding/dislocation/true syncope.
No deformed/stuck joint; intact neurovascular status.

differential.diagnosis: POTS secondary to hEDS - acute flare
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: POTS/hEDS flare needing urgent in-person ECG/joint review, fall-safety, and supportive binder/salt-fluids/bracing.

red_flags.description: Black/tarry stools, vomiting blood, or a supine BP drop (occult bleed/hypovolaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate significant bleeding needing emergency care.

red_flags.description: A joint dislocating and remaining deformed/stuck, or limb neurovascular compromise
red_flags.clinical_significance: A locked dislocation with neurovascular compromise is an orthopaedic emergency.

red_flags.description: True syncope, chest pain, or tachycardia persisting supine

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing everything to the known conditions without screening cardiac/bleeding/dislocation or arranging an ECG.
Starting rate-control/vasoactive drugs over chat before diagnosis.
Not addressing the very high fall risk from combined joint instability and orthostasis while isolated.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Postural HR rise >30 bpm; Beighton 7/9 (marked hypermobility)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports POTS secondary to hEDS with ECG confirmation, supportive measures and fall-safety.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Reliable postural vitals and cardiac auscultation
Joint examination/Beighton scoring and neurovascular assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen cardiac/bleeding/dislocation red flags; advise binder/salt-fluids/bracing/seated activities and fall-safety; arrange urgent in-person review (ECG + joints); escalate to 000 for GI bleeding, a locked dislocation, true syncope or chest pain.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing everything to POTS/hEDS and starting remote drugs could miss an arrhythmia/bleed/dislocation or leave a very high fall risk unmanaged.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Rhythm Society expert consensus on POTS
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: POTS (including with hEDS) is managed with compression/binders, volume expansion and reconditioning, with medications specialist-guided after an ECG.
Joint protection, bracing, physiotherapy and fall prevention are core; dislocations with neurovascular compromise are emergencies.
